purpose: Validate that ce-sessions findings preserve enough terminology resolution context for downstream vocabulary capture (load-bearing assumption in PR #838 — ce-compound Phase 2.4 scans ce-sessions findings for qualifying domain terms).
non_purpose: Not testing ce-sessions's general search quality or its ability to find sessions on arbitrary topics. The narrow assumption is about terminology-resolution preservation.

variance_protocol.stability_metric: stddev of must-tier term recall across runs

grading_pipeline.stage_1: Programmatic substring match per criticality tier (must / should / may). Pass = all 'must' terms appear in findings.
grading_pipeline.stage_2: LLM grader (see grader.md) — judges whether each 'expected_context' item is preserved WITH resolution rationale, not only as a keyword hit. Pass = all expected_context items receive 'preserved with context' verdict.

evals.name: synthesis-gate-recovery
evals.prompt: What was the synthesis gate work in ce-plan about? I want to understand how it was designed and what problems it solved.
expected_context: synthesis gate appears with its purpose (prevent silent proceed past synthesis without user check), not only as a keyword
Stated / Inferred / Out of scope appear as bucket categorization, not only as a phrase
evals.notes: Distinctive coined term that should be near-impossible to ignore if ce-sessions touched the originating session. Failure here indicates strong synthesis loss.

evals.name: mode-headless-semantic-alignment
evals.prompt: How was mode:headless aligned across the compound family of skills? Why was it added and what changed?

expected_terms.term: Compare per skill, not per mode

expected_context: mode:autofix → mode:headless rename appears with reasoning (the compound family should speak the same word)
process exhaust vs audit content principle appears with the refined rule (compare per skill, not per mode — interactive ce-compound doesn't validate the same inferences headless skips)
evals.notes: More nuanced than #1 — tests preservation of a multi-piece design decision (rename + cross-skill alignment + a principle refinement) rather than a single coined term.

evals.name: tangential-term-recovery
evals.prompt: I want to understand recent ce-plan workflow improvements — what's been done and why?
expected_context: synthesis gate surfaces despite the query being broader than the term itself — finding is not gated on the query containing 'synthesis' as a keyword
evals.notes: Tests whether ce-sessions surfaces tangentially-relevant terminology when the query is broader than the terms themselves. The query intentionally does NOT mention 'synthesis' — if the term is only retrievable by querying its own name, ce-sessions has an indexing gap for the vocabulary-capture use case (because ce-compound's Phase 2.4 won't query by term, it'll query by topic).

evals.name: near-miss-false-positive
evals.prompt: Were there any sessions on running services in headless mode for CI/CD or headless server deployments?
evals.expected_response_shape: either (a) 'no relevant sessions found' if the user has no sessions about server-headless topics, or (b) only sessions actually about CI/CD or server headless contexts — NOT the ce-compound mode:headless feature work

ground_truth.notes: PR #813 is about the compound-engineering mode:headless feature, not about server deployments. Finding that PR's session as a relevant result for this query would be a false positive.

evals.notes: Weaker signal than #1-3. If the user has no sessions about server-headless deployments, ce-sessions correctly returns 'no relevant' and the test trivially passes. Test only has discriminating signal if relevant unrelated sessions exist. Run anyway — a trivially-passing test still confirms ce-sessions doesn't return the ce-compound PR work as 'relevant' to a CI/CD deployment query.
